title: Combinational Circuits
summary: Combinational logic circuits, truth tables, half adders, and full adders.
content_html:
Combinational Circuits

A combinational circuit is a network of connected logic gates whose outputs depend only on the current values of its inputs — there is no memory, no feedback, and no history. The same set of inputs will always produce the same outputs. Combinational circuits form the foundation of arithmetic units, comparison logic, data routing, and many other essential computer functions.

Characteristics of Combinational Circuits

Combinational circuits have several defining properties:

  • No memory: Outputs are a pure function of current inputs. Previous inputs have no effect.
  • No feedback: No output is fed back to any input (that would create a sequential circuit).
  • Deterministic: Given the same inputs, the circuit always produces the same outputs.
  • Propagation delay: In real hardware, outputs take a small but nonzero time to respond to input changes, due to the physical delay of signals passing through gates.

Truth Tables

A truth table is a systematic listing of every possible combination of inputs alongside the corresponding outputs. For a circuit with n inputs, there are 2n rows in the truth table. Truth tables are the most direct way to specify or verify combinational logic.

Example truth table for a 2-input circuit with output Z = A AND B:

  • A=0, B=0 → Z=0
  • A=0, B=1 → Z=0
  • A=1, B=0 → Z=0
  • A=1, B=1 → Z=1

Half Adder

The half adder is a fundamental arithmetic circuit that adds two single-bit inputs (A and B) and produces two outputs: a Sum bit and a Carry bit.

  • Sum = A XOR B — the sum bit is 1 when exactly one input is 1
  • Carry = A AND B — the carry is 1 only when both inputs are 1 (because 1+1=10 in binary)

Truth table for the half adder:

  • A=0, B=0 → Sum=0, Carry=0 (0+0=0)
  • A=0, B=1 → Sum=1, Carry=0 (0+1=1)
  • A=1, B=0 → Sum=1, Carry=0 (1+0=1)
  • A=1, B=1 → Sum=0, Carry=1 (1+1=10 in binary)

The half adder is called “half” because it cannot process a carry-in from a previous stage, limiting its usefulness in multi-bit addition.

Full Adder

The full adder adds three single-bit inputs: A, B, and a carry-in (Cin). This makes it suitable for use in multi-bit ripple-carry adders where the carry from one bit position feeds into the next.

  • Sum = A XOR B XOR Cin
  • Carryout = A·B + Cin·(A XOR B)

A full adder can be constructed from two half adders and one OR gate:

  1. First half adder: computes A XOR B (partial sum) and A AND B (partial carry)
  2. Second half adder: adds partial sum with Cin, producing Sum and a second carry
  3. OR gate: combines the two carries into the final carry-out

By chaining n full adders, you can build an n-bit ripple-carry adder that adds two n-bit binary numbers. The carry “ripples” from the least significant bit to the most significant bit.

Circuit Diagrams and Conventions

When drawing logic circuits, standard conventions apply:

  • Wires carry voltage levels representing logical HIGH or LOW.
  • Gate outputs control the voltage on their output wires.
  • Inputs are typically shown on the left; outputs on the right.
  • Wires that cross without a dot are not connected; a dot indicates a junction.
  • Input sources include switches (manual), buttons, and clock signals (a square wave generator that alternates between HIGH and LOW at a fixed frequency).
  • Output indicators include LEDs (light when HIGH) and 7-segment displays.

Example: Cascaded AND-OR Circuit

Consider a circuit computing Z = (A AND B) OR (C AND D). This requires two AND gates followed by one OR gate:

  1. AND gate 1 computes A·B
  2. AND gate 2 computes C·D
  3. OR gate combines both results: Z = A·B + C·D

This structure, called a sum of products (SOP) form, appears very frequently in logic design. Any Boolean function can be expressed in SOP form, which maps directly to a two-level AND-OR circuit.

Practical Importance

Combinational circuits are used everywhere in modern processors. The Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) is a large combinational circuit that performs arithmetic and logical operations. Comparators, priority encoders, and multiplexers are all combinational. Even though modern computers also contain sequential elements (registers, memory), combinational logic is the engine of actual computation.

exercises.title: Half Adder Carry
exercises.prompt: What is the Carry output of a half adder when A=1 and B=1?

exercises.title: Full Adder Construction
exercises.prompt: How many half adders are needed to build one full adder?

exercises.title: Truth Table Rows
exercises.prompt: How many rows are in the truth table for a combinational circuit with 4 inputs?

exercises.title: Half Adder Sum Gate
exercises.prompt: Which gate computes the Sum output in a half adder?
